stem: You are the ED RMO seeing an 8-month-old infant brought in by her mother for worsening breathing. She has had 3 days of coryza and cough. Today the mother reports feeding has dropped to about 50% of usual volume and the last wet nappy was three hours ago. The infant is alert but distressed.

data_table.Vital signs: RR 62, SpO2 92% room air, HR 158, T 37.8 degrees C
data_table.Examination: Subcostal and intercostal recession, fine bilateral crackles, no wheeze, mucous membranes tacky

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Admit for inpatient observation with supplemental oxygen as needed and paced nasogastric feeds
options.rationale: Sustained SpO2 below 92% on room air, RR over 60, and feeding under 50% of normal meet RCH admission criteria for moderate bronchiolitis; supportive oxygen and paced feeds are first-line. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Trial inhaled salbutamol via spacer and formally reassess work of breathing after twenty minutes
options.rationale: Bronchodilators do not improve outcomes in typical viral bronchiolitis in infants under 12 months and are not recommended. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Administer a single oral prednisolone dose and observe in the emergency department for one hour
options.rationale: Systemic corticosteroids confer no benefit in acute bronchiolitis and are not used routinely. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Discharge home with written safety netting and a planned GP review the following morning
options.rationale: Discharge is unsafe with reduced feeding, reduced wet nappies, and borderline oxygenation, which together indicate moderate severity requiring admission. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Administer nebulised adrenaline 5 mg with a planned repeat dose at thirty minutes if needed
options.rationale: Nebulised adrenaline is not routinely recommended and does not change admission rates or length of stay. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

explanation.summary: Australian paediatric practice classifies bronchiolitis severity by oxygenation, respiratory effort, feeding, and hydration. Moderate severity meets admission criteria. Supportive care with oxygen and paced feeds is first-line; bronchodilators, steroids, and nebulised adrenaline are not recommended for typical viral bronchiolitis under 12 months. The discriminator here is recognising that feeding under 50% combined with borderline SpO2 mandates admission.
key_points: RCH admission triggers include SpO2 under 92%, RR over 60, feeds under half, or apnoeas
Bronchodilators, steroids, and adrenaline are not routinely indicated under 12 months
Paced NG feeds preserve hydration without increasing aspiration risk
explanation.pearls: RCH bronchiolitis pathway uses SpO2 below 92% as the supplemental oxygen threshold; do not start oxygen at higher saturations as it prolongs admission.

stem: You are the paediatric registrar reviewing a 10-month-old boy admitted overnight with first febrile UTI. Urine grew E. coli sensitive to cefalexin. He responded to IV ceftriaxone within 24 hours and has been afebrile for 48 hours. He is now feeding well, hydrated, and ready for discharge on oral cefalexin to complete the course. The parents ask about follow-up imaging.

data_table.Examination: Well, no abdominal mass, normal genitalia
data_table.Investigations: Urine culture E. coli sensitive to cefalexin; bedside renal tract ultrasound during admission was normal
data_table.Medications: Oral cefalexin to complete a total 10-day course

lead_in: What further imaging or investigation is most appropriate?

options.text: Arrange a DMSA scan at three months to assess for renal scarring after the first febrile UTI
options.rationale: DMSA is recommended after atypical or recurrent UTIs, not after a first uncomplicated febrile UTI with normal ultrasound. (Source: RCH CPG UTI)

options.text: Arrange a micturating cystourethrogram in the next four weeks to exclude vesicoureteric reflux
options.rationale: MCUG is reserved for atypical UTI, recurrent UTI, or abnormal ultrasound findings, not for a first uncomplicated UTI that responded promptly. (Source: RCH CPG UTI)

options.text: No further imaging is required as the renal tract ultrasound during admission was normal
options.rationale: For a first febrile UTI under 24 months with prompt response to first-line therapy and a normal ultrasound, no further imaging is required. (Source: RCH CPG UTI)

options.text: Commence trimethoprim antibiotic prophylaxis until two years of age as routine secondary prevention
options.rationale: Routine antibiotic prophylaxis after a single uncomplicated UTI is not recommended due to limited benefit and resistance. (Source: RCH CPG UTI)

options.text: Repeat a urine culture at the end of the antibiotic course to confirm microbiological cure
options.rationale: Test of cure is not required if the child is clinically well at the end of therapy. (Source: RCH CPG UTI)

explanation.summary: Australian guidance from RCH stratifies post-UTI imaging by atypia and recurrence. A first febrile UTI in a child under 24 months who responds promptly and has a normal renal ultrasound needs no further imaging. MCUG and DMSA are reserved for atypical features or recurrence. The distractors test over-investigation patterns common in inherited NICE-derived practice.
key_points: Renal tract ultrasound during admission is standard for febrile UTI under 24 months
Atypical features include non-E coli organism, poor response by 48 hours, abdominal mass, or sepsis
Prophylaxis is not first-line after a single uncomplicated UTI
explanation.pearls: RCH UTI pathway: ultrasound during admission for under-24-months febrile UTI; MCUG only if atypical or recurrent or ultrasound abnormal.

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic: UTI in children

stem: You are the PICU registrar caring for an 8-year-old girl 6 hours into treatment for severe diabetic ketoacidosis. She has received 10 mL/kg saline bolus and is on maintenance fluids with insulin infusion at 0.05 units/kg/hr. Her GCS was 15 on arrival. The nurse calls because the patient is now harder to rouse and complains of a headache, with her father noting she is unusually irritable when stimulated.

data_table.Examination: GCS 13 (E3 V4 M6), pupils 3 mm reactive, no focal neurology, abdomen soft
data_table.Investigations: Venous pH 7.18 (was 7.04), bicarbonate 14 (was 6), glucose 14 mmol/L, sodium 138, potassium 3.6
data_table.Medications: Insulin infusion 0.05 units/kg/hr; saline 0.9% with potassium replacement

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate action?

options.text: Switch from saline to compound sodium lactate solution and continue the current insulin infusion rate while monitoring closely
options.rationale: Changing crystalloid type does not treat cerebral oedema; osmotherapy is the time-critical intervention. (Source: RCH CPG DKA)

options.text: Stop the insulin infusion entirely because hypoglycaemia is the most likely cause of the decreased conscious state in this clinical context
options.rationale: Glucose is 14 mmol/L, so hypoglycaemia is excluded; stopping insulin would worsen ketosis without addressing cerebral oedema. (Source: RCH CPG DKA)

options.text: Increase the fluid replacement rate to correct presumed ongoing dehydration and add a further isotonic saline bolus over thirty minutes
options.rationale: Aggressive rehydration in established cerebral oedema worsens intracranial pressure and contradicts current PICU guidance. (Source: RCH CPG DKA)

options.text: Arrange an urgent CT brain before any treatment to confirm the diagnosis of cerebral oedema and exclude alternative intracranial pathology
options.rationale: Delaying osmotherapy for imaging worsens outcomes; treatment must precede imaging if cerebral oedema is clinically suspected. (Source: RCH CPG DKA)

options.text: Administer hypertonic saline or mannitol immediately and halve the maintenance fluid rate while obtaining urgent neuroimaging only after stabilisation
options.rationale: Bradycardia with rising blood pressure, falling GCS, and headache in a child being treated for DKA is cerebral oedema; treatment with osmotherapy and fluid reduction must precede imaging. (Source: RCH CPG DKA)

explanation.summary: Paediatric DKA cerebral oedema typically presents 4 to 12 hours into treatment with bradycardia, rising BP, falling GCS, and headache. Recognition is clinical and treatment must precede imaging. Hypertonic 3% saline (2.5 to 5 mL/kg) or mannitol 0.5 to 1 g/kg is first-line, with fluid rate reduction. Mortality remains 20 to 25% even with prompt treatment.
key_points: Cushing triad (bradycardia, hypertension, falling GCS) in DKA equals cerebral oedema until proven otherwise
Hypertonic saline 3% at 2.5 to 5 mL/kg or mannitol 0.5 to 1 g/kg is first-line osmotherapy
Imaging follows stabilisation, not the other way around
explanation.pearls: Risk factors for paediatric DKA cerebral oedema include younger age, severe acidosis, low pCO2, and rapid sodium correction; ISPAD recommends limiting initial bolus to 10 to 20 mL/kg in 1 to 2 hours.

sources.label: ISPAD Clinical Practice Consensus Guidelines DKA

stem: You are the paediatric registrar reviewing a 7-month-old boy with 6 days of fever, irritability, and limited oral intake. He has bilateral non-purulent conjunctival injection, cracked lips, and BCG site erythema, with no rash, no lymphadenopathy, and no peripheral changes. He has been treated empirically with oral antibiotics by the GP without improvement.

data_table.Examination: Conjunctival injection without exudate, dry cracked lips, BCG site erythema, no rash, no cervical lymphadenopathy, no peripheral oedema
data_table.Investigations: CRP 145 mg/L, ESR 78, platelets 540 x10^9/L, ALT 88, albumin 28, urine WCC raised without bacteriuria, sodium 132

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue supportive care and review tomorrow if no improvement, as classic criteria require five clinical features and have not yet been met
options.rationale: Waiting risks coronary aneurysm formation; supplementary laboratory criteria allow earlier diagnosis under 6 months when clinical features are sparse. (Source: RCH CPG Kawasaki)

options.text: Arrange echocardiography and commence IVIG 2 g/kg with aspirin based on the supplementary laboratory criteria for incomplete disease
options.rationale: Persistent fever beyond 5 days with 2 clinical features plus supportive labs (CRP raised, platelets raised, low albumin, low sodium, raised ALT, sterile pyuria) meets AHA incomplete Kawasaki criteria for treatment and urgent echocardiography. (Source: RCH CPG Kawasaki)

options.text: Change empiric antibiotics to IV ceftriaxone for occult bacterial sepsis after a full septic workup including lumbar puncture
options.rationale: Sterile pyuria, raised ALT, and low albumin in this pattern point to inflammatory rather than infectious aetiology in the prolonged fever picture. (Source: RCH CPG Kawasaki)

options.text: Arrange outpatient rheumatology review next week and commence oral prednisolone empirically for systemic juvenile idiopathic arthritis
options.rationale: sJIA requires quotidian fever pattern and evanescent rash, neither of which is present, and outpatient deferral risks aneurysms. (Source: RCH CPG Kawasaki)

options.text: Trial high-dose ibuprofen for presumed viral inflammation and reassess clinical features over the next forty-eight hours of observation
options.rationale: Delay risks coronary aneurysm; ibuprofen does not address the underlying vasculitis or replace IVIG. (Source: RCH CPG Kawasaki)

explanation.summary: Infants under 6 months frequently present with incomplete Kawasaki disease, with fewer than 4 of the 5 classic clinical features. The AHA 2017 algorithm allows treatment based on prolonged fever plus 2 clinical features and supplementary labs (CRP over 30, ESR over 40, plus 3 of: platelets over 450, low albumin, low sodium, raised ALT, sterile pyuria, WCC over 15). Early IVIG within 10 days reduces coronary aneurysm risk from 25% to under 5%. Echocardiography is mandatory at diagnosis.
key_points: Under 6 months age group has highest aneurysm risk despite often atypical presentation
AHA supplementary lab criteria allow earlier intervention in incomplete disease
IVIG 2 g/kg plus aspirin within 10 days reduces aneurysm risk substantially
explanation.pearls: BCG site reactivation is highly specific for Kawasaki disease in vaccinated infants; it is not in classical criteria but strongly supports the diagnosis.

stem: You are the paediatric registrar called to ED to see a 4-month-old infant brought in with episodes of unusual pallor and apparent fatigue over 12 hours. The parents describe two episodes of inconsolable crying lasting around 10 minutes followed by limpness, with no fever or vomiting. The child has had one normal stool today.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 162, RR 44, T 36.9, SpO2 99%, capillary refill 3 seconds
data_table.Examination: Pale, drowsy between episodes, abdomen soft with palpable fullness in right upper quadrant, normal genitalia
data_table.Investigations: Bedside glucose 4.8, FBC and UEC pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: CT abdomen with intravenous contrast to fully characterise abdominal pathology and assess for an underlying lead point causing the presentation
options.rationale: Ionising radiation in infants is avoided when ultrasound is diagnostic; CT has no first-line role for intussusception. (Source: RCH CPG Intussusception)

options.text: Three-view abdominal radiograph series to assess for free air and characterise any bowel obstruction before further imaging is arranged
options.rationale: Plain films may show non-specific signs but cannot reliably confirm or exclude intussusception; ultrasound is preferred first-line. (Source: RCH CPG Intussusception)

options.text: EEG to evaluate for atypical seizure activity given the recurrent episodes of decreased responsiveness reported by the parents over twelve hours
options.rationale: The pattern of inconsolable crying followed by limpness with a palpable mass is classic for intussusception, not seizure activity. (Source: RCH CPG Intussusception)

options.text: Urgent abdominal ultrasound to assess for a target sign with paediatric surgical consult booked in parallel
options.rationale: Pallor, intermittent inconsolable crying, lethargy, and a palpable mass in a 4-month-old strongly suggest intussusception; ultrasound is the first-line investigation with high sensitivity. (Source: RCH CPG Intussusception)

options.text: Lumbar puncture to exclude occult central nervous system infection causing the lethargy and unusual behaviour observed at home this morning
options.rationale: Without fever, irritability between episodes, or meningism, LP is not the priority and the clinical pattern is surgical. (Source: RCH CPG Intussusception)

explanation.summary: Intussusception classically presents at 3 months to 3 years with paroxysmal crying, drawing up of legs, pallor, lethargy between episodes, and a palpable sausage-shaped mass. Stool changes (redcurrant jelly) are late findings; their absence does not rule out the diagnosis. Ultrasound is highly sensitive and specific. Hydrostatic or pneumatic reduction is first-line management with surgery for failed reduction or perforation.
key_points: Lethargy between episodes is an under-recognised but specific feature in young infants
Ultrasound has sensitivity over 97% and is first-line
Air or contrast enema reduction is therapeutic in most cases without need for surgery
explanation.pearls: Pure lethargy in an infant can be the dominant presentation of intussusception, sometimes mistaken for sepsis or neurological illness; consider ultrasound when no clear cause is found.
